title: Knuth Shuffle and Reservoir Sampling

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Two related problems. (A) Generate a uniform random permutation of n items. (B) Sample one element uniformly from a stream of unknown length.
rules: Knuth shuffle: for i = 1 to n: swap A[i] with A[Random(i, n)]. Theta(n) time, Theta(1) extra memory.
Reservoir (k=1): keep current pick with prob n/(n+1) when reading the (n+1)-th element; replace with prob 1/(n+1).
Both rely on an inductive invariant -- after step i, every item processed so far has equal probability of being where it is.
setup.question: Why are these algorithms uniform?

learningObjectives: Prove Knuth shuffle by induction on i.
Prove reservoir sampling uniformity by induction on the stream length.
Recognize the wrong shuffle (uniform pick from full range each step) and why it's biased.

stages.label: Knuth shuffle correctness
stages.prompt: Why does 'for i=1 to n: swap A[i] with A[Random(i, n)]' produce a uniform permutation?

options.text: At step i, the chosen element is uniformly random among the n-i+1 elements still 'unfixed'. By induction, all (n-i+1)! orderings of the suffix are equally likely. So every full permutation has prob 1/n!.
Because the algorithm calls Random() exactly n times.
Because we shuffle the array twice.

stages.explanation: Inductive invariant: after step i, the prefix A[1..i] is a uniform random subset of i elements from the original n. Step i+1 picks uniformly from the n-i elements still in the suffix, extending the prefix uniformly.

stages.label: Wrong shuffle
stages.prompt: What's WRONG with 'for i=1 to n: swap A[i] with A[Random(1, n)]' (always sampling from the FULL range)?

options.text: There are n^n random sequences but only n! permutations. n^n is not divisible by n! for n >= 3, so the distribution is biased.
It's still uniform -- both versions work.

stages.explanation: n=3: 3^3 = 27 outcomes but 3! = 6 permutations. 27 / 6 = 4.5 -- not an integer, so the resulting distribution cannot be uniform. The Knuth fix (sample from i..n) gives exactly n! outcomes.

stages.label: Commit -- reservoir induction
stages.prompt: Reservoir sampling with k=1. After reading n elements, each has prob 1/n of being kept. Now element n+1 arrives. What's the rule and why?

options.text: Replace current with prob 1/(n+1). Then each prior element has prob (1/n) * (n/(n+1)) = 1/(n+1) of being kept; the new one is kept with prob 1/(n+1). Uniform.
Always replace with the new element.
Never replace once a pick is made.

stages.explanation: Replace-prob 1/(n+1) is the only rule that preserves uniformity. Memory: Theta(1). Algorithm: idx = 1; for n = 2..end: with prob 1/n, idx = n. The current pick after the stream ends is uniform over the stream.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize reservoir sampling to k > 1 (e.g., select k uniformly random items from a stream).
What's the variance of the rank of the kept item under reservoir sampling? (Compute it.)
Why is sort-by-random-key Theta(n log n) but Knuth Theta(n)? (Knuth makes one pass; sort needs log n comparisons per element.)
